title: Sitka’s superintendent says school enrollment is up this year
summary: Enrollment numbers are looking up, slightly, for the Sitka School District. When the Sitka School Board met on November 5, Superintendent Deidre Jenson said the final student count will go to the state this week, where the data will be reviewed to ensure there are no duplicates. “That takes a while...

title: Governor Dunleavy Extends Disaster Declaration for West Coast Storms
summary: Today Governor Mike Dunleavy signed an extension of the Disaster Declaration for the October 2025 West Coast Storms. The 30-day extension will enable recovery efforts to continue unincumbered so that the maximize amount of work can be completed before the onset of the winter freeze up in the region....

title: AST DUI: Fairbanks [AK25112830]
summary: On November 8, 2025, at approximately 14:36 hours, AST received a REDDI report involving a driver on the Steese HWY. The apprehended and remanded to the Fairbanks Correctional Facility for DUI. The investigation is ongoing.
sourceAttribution: Source: Alaska State Troopers Daily Dispatch

title: Climate threats could cost Alaska billions. After October’s storm, advocates say it’s time to act.
summary: Homes and other buildings in the Western Alaska village of Kwigillingok were lifted off their foundations during last month's storm; some were floated across a river and one came to rest over a small creek. (Nathaniel Herz/KYUK and Northern Journal) After a remnant typhoon hammered Indigenous communities in Western Alaska last month, the state’s elected leaders have been fielding questions about how to protect the region that faces growing threats from storms as the climate warms. At a news conference last week, Gov. Mike Dunleavy answered the question with one of his own. “It’s a complex question to get the answers to. Not only: What do you do to make a community prepared for the future? But: Where do you get the money and how do you do that?” he said. In fact, that question had already been answered, in a report released a year earlier. The 200-page document was published by the Alaska Native Tribal Health Consortium with contributions from Dunleavy’s own administration. And it conta...

title: AST MVC: North Pole [AK25112828]
summary: On November 8, 2025, at approximately 2:50 p.m., a southbound 2002 Chevrolet S10 driven by Joshua Davis, 21, of Fort Wainwright, lost control near mile 352 of the Richardson Highway, crossed the median, and struck a northbound Alaska State Trooper patrol vehicle head-on. No major injuries were repor...
sourceAttribution: Source: Alaska State Troopers Daily Dispatch

title: AST Death Investigation: Fairbanks [AK25112817]
summary: On 11/08/2025, AST received a report that 30-year-old Cory Wicken of Fairbanks had been found deceased. The State Medical examiner has requested the body to determine cause of death. Next of kin has been notified.
sourceAttribution: Source: Alaska State Troopers Daily Dispatch

summary: Cancer Connection’s annual Community Health Forum, held on October 25th, featured an inspiring and informative presentation on “State-of-the-Art Cancer Care: Hope, Innovation, and Humanity in Oncology.” The presenter, Prakash Vishnu, MD, FACP, engaged the audience in a fascinating review of the prog...

title: Alaska has highest concentration of military veterans in the U.S.
summary: Soldiers are a signature of Alaska life, where about 8% of all residents (roughly 58,000) are military veterans – the highest concentration in the country. Nationwide, only about 5% of the population served in the military. A new report from Alaska’s Dept. of Labor notes that large military bases in...

title: AST Warrant Arrest: Wasilla [AK25107959]
summary: On 11/07/2025 at approximately 2130 hours, while conducting a separate investigation, Alaska State Troopers contacted James Auld, age 21 of Wasilla. James had an active arrest warrant (3PA-24-00914CR, Failure to Remand, O/C - DUI) and was arrested and remanded to Mat-Su Pretrial without incident.

title: ‘This gives me a little control’; Ketchikan organizations respond to SNAP delays
summary: Jennifer Tucker is the manager of Outpost Agriculture. The hydroponic company is offering free greens to Ketchikan’s SNAP recipients and furloughed federal workers. (Hunter Morrison/KRBD) Jennifer Tucker stands between rows of romaine lettuce she grew inside of a climate-controlled shipping container. She grabs a bundle by the head and gives it a pull. “So these will be harvested on Monday, and they will double in size between Friday and Monday,” Tucker explained. Tucker is offering free greens to Ketchikan’s SNAP recipients and furlo...

title: AST Domestic Violence: Wasilla [AK25112712]
summary: On 11/08/2025 at approximately 0040 hours, Alaska State Troopers responded to a disturbance in Wasilla. Following the investigation, Kenny Wells, age 38 of Wasilla, was arrested for domestic violence-related assault in the fourth degree, criminal mischief in the fourth degree, and unlawful contact in the second degree. Wells was remanded to Mat-Su Pretrial and held without bail.
sourceAttribution: Source: Alaska State Troopers Daily Dispatch

charges: domestic violence-related assault in the fourth degree
criminal mischief in the fourth degree
unlawful contact in the second degree

title: Sitka police say Thursday car crash likely caused by ‘medical issue’
summary: Sitka police say a crash involving two vehicles and a food truck near Sitka’s roundabout Thursday (11-6-25) was likely caused by a medical issue. Sitka Police Department spokesperson Serena Wild said shortly before 1:30 p.m., the department received multiple 911 calls reporting the accident on Lake...

title: Southeast drug enforcement task force seizes 4.5 pounds of methamphetamine
summary: The Ketchikan Police Department has seized about 4.5 pounds of methamphetamine that was shipped by mail to Ketchikan. Investigators with the regional drug enforcement task force, Southeast Alaska Cities Against Drugs, or SEACAD, identified a suspicious package late last week. That ended up being $31...

title: Alaska parole rates are among the lowest in the nation. Advocates want to know why.
summary: The entrance to the Anchorage Correctional Complex is seen on Aug. 29, 2022. (Photo by Yereth Rosen/Alaska Beacon) The number of people approved for discretionary parole by the Alaska State Board of Parole has dropped significantly in recent years, and advocates are asking the board for explanations. Last year, the board denied 59% of applicants for discretionary parole. In 2023, it denied 58% of applicants. In 2022, it denied 70%. An analysis by the Prison Policy Initiative, a non-profit research group, found that from 2019 to 2022, Alaska reduced the number of people released through discretionary parole by 79% — the largest percent change nationwide. In years prior, the grant rate was much higher : Only 25% of applicants were denied parole in 2015, and 30%...
